summary: Gruber analyzes Google's live Duplex demonstrations for journalists, which followed the controversial I/O keynote demo that many suspected was fake. While the technology impressively mimics human speech patterns, Gruber argues the real breakthrough is how human-like Duplex sounds, not how smart it is — as demonstrated by a journalist who tripped up Duplex's logic but couldn't tell when a human operator took over. He questions the ethics of injecting 'speech disfluencies' like 'um' and 'ah' to prevent hangups, noting it's disingenuous to claim these aren't meant to trick people. Gruber is deeply skeptical that Duplex will become a real product, citing its 20 percent failure rate and the army of human operators that would require at scale. He concludes it's a technology demo masquerading as a product, and that Google would be better off building an OpenTable competitor.
key_points: Google's Duplex breakthrough is in human-like speech synthesis, not conversational intelligence — journalists could confuse its logic but couldn't distinguish it from a real human voice

Google's VP conceded the I/O demo 'maybe made it look a little too polished,' and the injected speech disfluencies exist specifically to prevent hangups, making the claim they're not meant to trick people disingenuous
A 20 percent failure rate requiring human operator intervention makes Duplex untenable as a shipping product at Google's scale
Duplex is a technology demo pitched as an imminent product because it made for a stunning keynote moment — Google itself admits it's 'somewhere between technology demo and product'
If Google truly wanted to solve restaurant reservations, building a free OpenTable competitor for small businesses would be far more practical
money_quotes: Google's breakthrough isn't how smart Duplex is, but how human-like it sounds.
It's disingenuous to say they're not using these um's and ah's to trick the person into thinking it's a human. That's exactly what they're doing.
The problem is, tricking sounds devious. I'm not sure it is in this case. It's just making the person on the call more comfortable.
I still think the whole thing feels like a demo of a technology (the human-like speech), not a product.
With a 20 percent failure rate, Google would need an army of human operators standing by all day long, to support a feature they don't make any money from.
Right now it feels like a feature in search of a product, but they pitched it as an imminent product at I/O because it made for a stunning demo.
To me, the truly difficult ethical questions are years down the road, when these AI's get close to passing an open-ended Turing test.
I'm not holding my breath for Duplex ever to allow anyone to make a reservation at any establishment.
key_insight: Google Duplex is a genuinely impressive speech synthesis demo masquerading as a product, but its 20 percent failure rate and lack of a business model make it a technology in search of a use case rather than a viable feature.
